definition: Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC) is a structured process for planning, building, testing, deploying, and maintaining software.
advantages: Clear phases and planning; improves quality control; supports documentation and management.
disadvantages: Traditional models can be rigid; can be slow for changing requirements.
real_world_use: Used in enterprise software development to ensure predictable delivery and compliance.
example: Building a banking system using phases: requirements ? design ? coding ? testing ? deployment.
interview_questions: What are SDLC phases?; Why is SDLC important?; SDLC vs Agile?

definition: Waterfall is a linear SDLC model where each phase completes before the next begins.
advantages: Simple and easy to manage; strong documentation; good for stable requirements.
disadvantages: Hard to change after a phase; late testing; risks discovered late.
real_world_use: Used in government/regulated projects with fixed requirements.
example: A payroll system with fixed requirements and formal sign-off each stage.
interview_questions: When is Waterfall suitable?; Waterfall limitations?; Waterfall vs Agile?

definition: Agile is an iterative approach that delivers software in small increments with frequent feedback.
advantages: Fast delivery; adapts to change; continuous improvement; better customer collaboration.
disadvantages: Needs active stakeholder involvement; scope can expand; requires mature team discipline.
real_world_use: Used in startups and product teams where requirements evolve quickly.
example: Developing an app with 2-week sprints delivering working features each sprint.
interview_questions: What are Agile principles?; Agile vs Waterfall?; What is iteration?

definition: Scrum is an Agile framework using sprints, roles, and ceremonies to deliver increments of value.
advantages: Clear roles; predictable sprint cycles; strong transparency and feedback.
disadvantages: Needs strong team commitment; can be misused as micromanagement; depends on good backlog.
real_world_use: Used in software product teams delivering features every sprint.
example: Sprint planning ? daily scrum ? sprint review ? retrospective for an e-commerce feature.
interview_questions: Scrum roles?; What is a sprint?; What is a product backlog?

definition: Kanban is a workflow method that visualizes work and limits work-in-progress (WIP) to improve flow.
advantages: Improves flow; reduces bottlenecks; flexible priorities; continuous delivery.
disadvantages: Less structured time-boxing; may lack planning if unmanaged.
real_world_use: Used in support teams, DevOps, and continuous delivery pipelines.
example: A board with To Do ? Doing (WIP=3) ? Done for bug fixes.
interview_questions: What is WIP limit?; Kanban vs Scrum?; How does Kanban improve flow?

definition: DevOps is a culture and set of practices that unifies development and operations to deliver software faster and more reliably.
advantages: Faster releases; better reliability; automation; improved collaboration.
disadvantages: Tooling complexity; culture change needed; requires monitoring discipline.
real_world_use: Used in cloud-native systems to automate build/test/deploy and monitoring.
example: CI pipeline builds and tests on every commit, CD deploys to staging/production.
interview_questions: What is CI/CD?; DevOps benefits?; DevOps tools?

definition: CI/CD automates integration, testing, and deployment so changes can be delivered frequently and safely.
advantages: Fewer integration issues; faster feedback; reliable releases; automation reduces errors.
disadvantages: Setup cost; requires good tests; pipeline failures can block delivery.
real_world_use: Used in modern development with Git workflows and automated deployment.
example: GitHub Actions runs tests and deploys to cloud on merge to main.
interview_questions: CI vs CD?; Why automated tests matter?; What is a pipeline?

definition: Requirements engineering is the process of eliciting, analyzing, documenting, and validating what a system must do.
advantages: Reduces misunderstandings; improves planning; helps testing and acceptance.
disadvantages: Stakeholders may be unclear; requirements can change frequently.
real_world_use: Used in all projects to define scope and acceptance criteria.
example: Writing user stories and acceptance criteria for login feature.
interview_questions: Functional vs non-functional requirements?; What is elicitation?; What is validation?

definition: Functional requirements describe what the system should do (features, behaviors).
advantages: Defines clear features; supports test case design.
disadvantages: Can miss quality aspects if only functional is captured.
real_world_use: Used in specification documents and user stories.
example: �User can reset password via email link.�
interview_questions: Give examples of functional requirements; How to validate them?

definition: Non-functional requirements define quality attributes like performance, security, usability, and reliability.
advantages: Ensures quality; prevents late surprises; guides architecture.
disadvantages: Harder to measure; often ignored until late.
real_world_use: Used to set SLAs and design constraints.
example: �API must respond within 200ms for 95% requests.�
interview_questions: Examples of NFRs?; Why NFRs important?; How to measure performance?

definition: User stories are simple descriptions of features from the user�s perspective, often formatted as: As a <user>, I want <goal>, so that <benefit>.
advantages: Easy to understand; supports Agile planning; focuses on user value.
disadvantages: May be too high-level; needs acceptance criteria.
real_world_use: Used in Agile backlogs for feature planning.
example: As a customer, I want to track my order so I feel informed.
interview_questions: What is a good user story?; What is acceptance criteria?; INVEST principle?

definition: Use case modeling captures system interactions with actors to describe functional behavior.
advantages: Clarifies interactions; helps scope; supports UML diagrams.
disadvantages: Can become large; needs maintenance with changes.
real_world_use: Used in analysis for business systems and requirements.
example: Use case: �Place Order� with actor �Customer�.
interview_questions: What is an actor?; Include vs extend?; Use case vs user story?

definition: UML (Unified Modeling Language) is a standard set of diagrams for visualizing and documenting software systems.
advantages: Improves communication; helps design; supports documentation.
disadvantages: Can be time-consuming; diagrams can become outdated.
real_world_use: Used in system design, documentation, and architecture discussions.
example: Using class and sequence diagrams for order processing module.
interview_questions: Why use UML?; Common UML diagrams?; When not to over-document?

definition: A use case diagram shows actors and their interactions (use cases) with the system.
disadvantages: Not detailed; needs use case descriptions.
example: Actors: Student, Admin; Use cases: Login, Take Quiz.
interview_questions: Include vs extend?; What does a use case represent?

definition: A class diagram shows classes, attributes, methods, and relationships (inheritance, association).
disadvantages: May miss runtime behavior; can be complex.
example: Classes: User, Quiz, Attempt with relationships.
interview_questions: Association vs aggregation vs composition?; What is inheritance?

definition: A sequence diagram shows object interactions over time (messages).
advantages: Clarifies flow; good for APIs and processes.
disadvantages: Can be large; requires updates when logic changes.
example: Login: UI ? API ? DB ? API ? UI.
interview_questions: What is lifeline?; What is synchronous vs asynchronous message?

definition: An activity diagram models workflows and decision points.
advantages: Great for business flows; shows branching and parallel steps.
disadvantages: May become complex for large workflows.
example: Checkout process with decisions for payment success/failure.
interview_questions: When to use activity diagram?; How to model decisions?

definition: An ER diagram models database entities, attributes, and relationships.
disadvantages: Needs normalization knowledge; changes require updates.
example: Entities: Student, Course, Enrollment.
interview_questions: One-to-many vs many-to-many?; What is primary key?

definition: Normalization organizes tables to reduce redundancy and improve data integrity (1NF, 2NF, 3NF).
advantages: Less duplication; fewer anomalies; better consistency.
disadvantages: More joins; can reduce read performance.
example: Splitting user_address into separate table to avoid repeating addresses.
interview_questions: What are anomalies?; Explain 1NF/2NF/3NF; When to denormalize?

definition: SQL joins combine rows from tables based on related keys (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL).
disadvantages: Complex queries; performance issues if unindexed.
example: Join users and quiz_attempts to show user scores.
interview_questions: INNER vs LEFT join?; When to use joins?; Index impact?

definition: REST is an architectural style for building web APIs using HTTP methods and stateless operations.
advantages: Simple and scalable; widely adopted; uses standard HTTP.
disadvantages: Requires good design; versioning challenges.
example: GET /api/quiz/start?topic=SDLC
interview_questions: What is stateless?; Idempotency?; REST vs SOAP?

definition: HTTP methods define actions: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE.
disadvantages: Misuse causes confusion; security must be enforced.
example: POST /auth/login; GET /progress
interview_questions: PUT vs PATCH?; Which is idempotent?; Safe methods?

definition: Authentication verifies identity (login) using passwords, tokens, OAuth, etc.
disadvantages: Adds complexity; token storage risks.
example: JWT login for student portal.
interview_questions: Session vs JWT?; Where to store tokens?; What is OAuth?

definition: Authorization determines what an authenticated user can access based on roles/permissions.
disadvantages: Role explosion if unmanaged; needs careful design.
real_world_use: Used for admin dashboards and protected routes.
example: Admin can delete users; student cannot.
interview_questions: AuthN vs AuthZ?; RBAC?; How to enforce on backend?

definition: JWT is a token format used to securely transmit claims (user id/role) between client and server.
disadvantages: Token revocation hard; must secure secrets; expiry needed.
example: Store JWT and send in Authorization header.
interview_questions: What is a JWT structure?; How to secure JWT?; Refresh tokens?

definition: Role-Based Access Control assigns permissions based on roles like admin, student.
disadvantages: Too coarse for complex systems.
example: Roles: admin, student; middleware requireAdmin.
interview_questions: RBAC vs ABAC?; How to prevent privilege escalation?

definition: Software architecture is the high-level structure of a system, its components, and how they interact.
advantages: Guides scalability; improves maintainability; helps planning.
disadvantages: Wrong architecture is costly; needs experience.
example: Choosing microservices vs monolith for a platform.
interview_questions: What are architectural styles?; What is trade-off analysis?

definition: A monolith is a single deployable application containing many modules.
disadvantages: Hard to scale parts independently; large codebase complexity.
real_world_use: Used in small/medium apps or early stages.
example: Single Node.js API handling auth, chat, quiz, admin.
interview_questions: When to choose monolith?; Scaling monolith?; Monolith vs microservices?

definition: Microservices split a system into small independent services communicating over APIs.
advantages: Independent scaling; team autonomy; fault isolation.
disadvantages: Distributed complexity; monitoring and DevOps needs.
real_world_use: Used in large systems with many teams.
example: Auth service, Quiz service, Recommendation service separately.
interview_questions: Microservices pros/cons?; Data consistency?; Service communication?

definition: Design patterns are reusable solutions to common design problems (e.g., Singleton, Factory, Observer).
disadvantages: Overuse adds complexity.
example: Factory to create notification providers (email/sms).
interview_questions: What is Factory pattern?; Observer use case?; Anti-patterns?

definition: MVC separates application into Model, View, Controller to organize code.
disadvantages: Can be overkill for small apps.
real_world_use: Used in web frameworks and apps.
example: React UI (View) + API (Controller) + DB models.
interview_questions: What is MVC?; MVC in React?; Why separation helps?

definition: SOLID are 5 OOP principles: SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP.
disadvantages: Needs practice; can be misunderstood.
real_world_use: Used in OOP design and code reviews.
example: Applying SRP by separating validation and persistence logic.
interview_questions: Explain SRP?; What is DIP?; Benefits of SOLID?

definition: Git is a distributed version control system for tracking code changes and collaboration.
disadvantages: Merge conflicts; learning curve.
real_world_use: Used in almost all software projects.
example: Feature branch workflow with pull requests.
interview_questions: What is commit?; What is merge conflict?; Git rebase vs merge?

definition: Branching strategy defines how teams use branches (main, develop, feature, release).
disadvantages: Too many branches complicate workflow.
real_world_use: Used in team projects and CI/CD.
example: GitFlow or trunk-based development.
interview_questions: GitFlow vs trunk-based?; Why PR reviews?

definition: Code review is the practice of reviewing code changes before merging.
advantages: Improves quality; knowledge sharing; reduces bugs.
disadvantages: Slows delivery if process heavy.
example: Review PR for auth middleware and routes.
interview_questions: What to check in code review?; Best practices?

definition: Unit testing tests small parts of code (functions/classes) in isolation.
advantages: Fast feedback; catches regressions; supports refactoring.
disadvantages: Requires writing tests; mocking complexity.
example: Test score calculation function with jest/pytest.
interview_questions: What makes a good unit test?; Mocking?; AAA pattern?

definition: Integration testing checks interactions between modules/services.
advantages: Catches API contract issues; more realistic.
disadvantages: Slower than unit tests; environment setup.
example: Test /quiz/submit with a test DB.
interview_questions: Unit vs integration tests?; Test data management?

definition: System testing tests the complete application end-to-end.
disadvantages: Slow; brittle; needs stable environment.
example: Login ? chat ? quiz ? progress flow test.
interview_questions: E2E vs system test?; When to run system tests?

definition: Regression testing ensures changes didn�t break existing features.
disadvantages: Test suites can grow and slow down.
example: After adding theory quiz, ensure normal quiz still works.
interview_questions: What is regression?; How to automate regression?

definition: Test case design creates cases with inputs, steps, expected outputs.
disadvantages: Time-consuming; needs updates when requirements change.
example: Test login with valid/invalid credentials.
interview_questions: Boundary value analysis?; Equivalence partitioning?

definition: Debugging is finding and fixing defects using logs, breakpoints, and analysis.
disadvantages: Time-consuming without good tools.
example: Using console logs and debugger to trace 401 errors.
interview_questions: How to debug Node errors?; Logging best practices?

definition: Error handling ensures failures are caught and meaningful messages are returned.
disadvantages: If done poorly can hide issues.
example: Return 400 for bad input; 500 for server errors.
interview_questions: 400 vs 500?; How to design API errors?

definition: Logging records events; monitoring tracks system health and alerts.
disadvantages: Storage cost; needs discipline.
example: Monitor API latency and error rate.
interview_questions: What to log?; Metrics vs logs?; Alerts?

definition: Performance optimization improves speed and resource usage (CPU, memory, DB queries).
disadvantages: Premature optimization can waste time.
example: Index DB fields used in queries.
interview_questions: How to profile performance?; DB indexing?; Caching?

definition: Caching stores frequently used data to reduce repeated work.
disadvantages: Cache invalidation complexity.
real_world_use: Used in web apps and APIs.
example: Cache lessons/resources to avoid re-reading CSV repeatedly.
interview_questions: What is cache invalidation?; Where to cache?

definition: Concurrency means handling multiple tasks at the same time (threads/async).
disadvantages: Race conditions; complexity.
real_world_use: Used in servers and async operations.
example: Node handles concurrent requests using event loop.
interview_questions: What is async?; Race condition?; Deadlock?

definition: Security fundamentals include input validation, authentication, authorization, encryption, and secure coding.
disadvantages: Requires constant attention; evolving threats.
example: Hash passwords with bcrypt, validate inputs, use HTTPS.
interview_questions: SQL injection?; XSS?; CSRF?

definition: OWASP Top 10 is a list of common web application security risks.
disadvantages: Requires effort to implement mitigations.
example: Prevent injection by validation and parameterized queries.
interview_questions: Name OWASP risks?; How to prevent XSS?

definition: SQL injection is an attack where malicious SQL is inserted into queries.
advantages: Can lead to data theft and damage.
disadvantages: Happens if inputs are not handled safely.
real_world_use: Used in security education and prevention.
example: Using parameterized queries prevents injection.
interview_questions: What is SQL injection?; How to prevent it?

definition: Cross-site scripting (XSS) injects malicious scripts into web pages.
disadvantages: Occurs if untrusted input is rendered unsafely.
example: Escape output and avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML.
interview_questions: Stored vs reflected XSS?; How to prevent XSS?

definition: CSRF tricks a user�s browser into making unwanted requests.
advantages: Can perform actions without user intent.
disadvantages: Needs anti-CSRF tokens and same-site cookies.
example: CSRF token in forms for state-changing requests.
interview_questions: What is CSRF?; How to prevent it?

definition: Software maintenance includes fixing bugs, improving performance, and adapting to new requirements.
advantages: Keeps systems useful; improves quality over time.
disadvantages: Can be costly; legacy constraints.
example: Refactor old module to improve readability and tests.
interview_questions: Types of maintenance?; Refactoring vs rewriting?

definition: Refactoring is improving code structure without changing behavior.
disadvantages: Risk if tests are missing.
example: Extract function, rename variables, remove duplication.
interview_questions: What is refactoring?; Safe refactoring steps?

definition: Technical debt is the cost of quick/poor solutions that require future rework.
disadvantages: Hard to measure precisely.
example: Skipping tests now causes bugs later and slows releases.
interview_questions: Examples of technical debt?; How to reduce it?

definition: Project estimation predicts effort/time using techniques like story points, planning poker.
disadvantages: Often inaccurate; uncertainty high.
real_world_use: Used in Agile planning and project management.
example: Estimate user story complexity and assign points.
interview_questions: Story points vs hours?; Planning poker?

definition: Risk management identifies and mitigates project risks.
disadvantages: Needs continuous review.
example: Risk: key developer leaves; mitigation: documentation and pairing.
interview_questions: Risk vs issue?; Risk mitigation strategies?

definition: Software metrics measure quality and productivity (defect density, velocity, coverage).
disadvantages: Metrics can be gamed; may mislead.
example: Tracking test coverage and bug rates per release.
interview_questions: What is defect density?; What is velocity?; Pros/cons of metrics?

definition: Deployment is releasing software to an environment (staging/production) safely.
disadvantages: Risk of downtime; needs rollback plan.
example: Blue-green deployment to reduce downtime.
interview_questions: Deployment strategies?; Rollback plan?; Staging vs production?

definition: Release management plans, schedules, and controls moving releases to production.
disadvantages: Process overhead if too heavy.
real_world_use: Used in organizations with multiple teams.
example: Release notes and approval process for production release.
interview_questions: What is release vs deployment?; Versioning strategies?
